message:
🐛 Fix large clusters showing offline by parallelizing health checks (#856)

* 🐛 Fix large clusters showing offline by parallelizing health checks

The health check for large clusters (e.g. pok-prod-sa with 18 nodes,
972 pods) was timing out because nodes, pods, and PVCs were fetched
sequentially within a single 30s context deadline. This change:

- Parallelizes nodes/pods/PVCs fetches in GetClusterHealth() using
  goroutines, reducing wall-clock time from sum to max of individual
- Treats agent timeout errors as transient in the frontend, keeping
  cached data instead of immediately marking clusters offline
- Only marks clusters offline after definitive errors (connection
  refused, no such host, cert/auth errors) or 5+ minutes of failures

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Anderson <andy@clubanderson.com>

* 🐛 Fix agent caching failures and increase timeouts for large clusters

Additional fixes for large cluster health checks:

- Use background context in agent handler so health checks complete
  even if the frontend disconnects (prevents "context canceled" errors)
- Only cache successful health results — failures are not cached so
  the next request retries immediately
- Increase handler timeout to 60s and REST client timeout to 45s for
  clusters with slow API servers (pok-prod-sa nodes call takes 10-30s)
- Increase frontend fetch timeout to 65s to match
- Add useMinSpin hook to guarantee refresh spinner completes at least
  1 full rotation (1s) even when cached data returns instantly

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Anderson <andy@clubanderson.com>

* 🐛 Preserve cached pod/PVC data when listing times out

When pods or PVCs listing times out on large clusters but nodes
succeeds, fall back to previously cached pod/PVC values instead
of showing 0. This prevents the display from flickering between
real counts and zero on clusters where pod listing is slow.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Anderson <andy@clubanderson.com>

* 🐛 Fix useMinSpin hook to reliably guarantee 1 full rotation

The previous implementation depended on both refreshing and spinning
in its effect deps, causing the cleanup to clear the timer when
spinning state changed. Now:
- Only depends on refreshing to avoid re-trigger loops
- Tracks spin start time and calculates remaining duration
- Separate unmount cleanup

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Anderson <andy@clubanderson.com>

* 🐛 Fix useMinSpin stale closure with ref-based state tracking

Use spinningRef to track spinning state instead of relying on the
closure value, which can be stale when refreshing transitions
true→false faster than React commits the spinning state update.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Anderson <andy@clubanderson.com>

---------

Signed-off-by: Andrew Anderson <andy@clubanderson.com>
Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -1094,7 +1094,10 @@ func (s *Server) handleClusterHealthHTTP(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request)
 		return
 	}
 
-	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(r.Context(), 30*time.Second)
+	// Use background context instead of request context so the health check
+	// continues even if the frontend disconnects. Results are cached, so
+	// completing the check benefits subsequent requests.
+	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 60*time.Second)
 	defer cancel()
 
 	health, err := s.k8sClient.GetClusterHealth(ctx, cluster)
